title: Global cooperation on nuclear disarmament looks even further away
gist: The 2026 NPT Review Conference failed for the third consecutive time, but unlike 2022 (when Russia alone blocked consensus) this year saw multiple P5 states obstruct progress, and with New START expired and nothing replacing it, the US and Russia have no agreed nuclear arms limits for the first time in over fifty years.

themes.theme: A third consecutive NPT failure, and a broader one

insights.claim: The 2026 NPT Review Conference concluded on 22 May without a consensus outcome document, the third time in a row that states parties have failed to agree on a review of the treaty's implementation or a plan to strengthen it.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Three consecutive failed review cycles signals a structural, not one-off, breakdown in the nuclear non-proliferation consensus process.
insights.locator: The 2026 Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons

insights.claim: This year's failure differed from 2022, when Russia alone blocked agreement following its invasion of Ukraine: in 2026 multiple P5 countries were prepared to hinder progress, indicating not a targeted disruption but a non-proliferation system under strain in an increasingly dysfunctional environment.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A multi-actor breakdown is harder to fix than a single-spoiler problem, implying the NPT process faces a deeper structural crisis than in 2022.
insights.locator: In 2022, it was Russia alone that blocked agreement

insights.claim: The primary cause of the conference's failure was disagreement over adding a paragraph addressing Iran's non-compliance with its NPT obligations and stating Iran could never acquire nuclear weapons, which remained bracketed in the final draft, meaning no consensus was reached.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The still-unresolved Iran non-compliance language is a direct, trackable flashpoint that will resurface at future non-proliferation forums.
insights.locator: The primary cause of failure was the Iran conflict

insights.claim: When Conference President Đỗ Hùng Việt asked delegates to adopt at least a procedural consensus on strengthening the review process, Russia, China and Iran blocked even that.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Blocking even a minimal procedural fallback shows the obstruction went beyond substantive disagreement into rejecting the process itself.
insights.locator: When he asked the conference to adopt at least a procedural consensus

insights.claim: Other fault lines included Russia pushing for deletion of text on North Korea's weapons programme (prompting South Korean objections) and unresolved disputes over language on Ukraine's Russian-occupied Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant, reflecting a pattern where review conferences become forums for airing regional and bilateral grievances.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: When powerful states prefer bilateral leverage over collective frameworks, consensus-based multilateral non-proliferation processes become structurally difficult to sustain going forward.
insights.locator: Russia pushed for the deletion of text on North Korea’s weapons programme

insights.claim: New START, the US-Russia nuclear arms control agreement, expired in February 2026 with nothing to replace it, leaving the world's two largest nuclear arsenals without any agreed limits for the first time in over fifty years.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The absence of any US-Russia arms limits for the first time in 50+ years is a first-order geopolitical risk marker, independent of the NPT conference outcome itself.
insights.locator: New START, a US–Russia nuclear arms control agreement, expired in February

insights.claim: China's nuclear build-up is accelerating, the US has threatened to resume nuclear testing and accused both Russia and China of conducting tests, and France has announced an expansion of its nuclear programme.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Simultaneous build-up/testing signals from China, the US and France point to a broadening multilateral arms race, not just a US-Russia bilateral issue.

insights.claim: In this environment, the five recognized nuclear weapon states arrived in New York and forced the removal of language calling on them to begin negotiations on disarmament or even to pursue discussions urgently, also stripping even mild requests for greater transparency and accountability, leaving only a vague call for 'constructive dialogue'.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The P5's active removal of disarmament and transparency language (not just failure to add it) is a stronger, more deliberate signal of retreat from disarmament commitments than passive inaction would be.
insights.quote: constructive dialogue that might facilitate future progress
insights.locator: Nuclear weapons states removed even more mild requests

insights.claim: Many non-nuclear weapons states will interpret the weakened final draft as a signal that, beyond preventing other countries from acquiring nuclear weapons, the P5 are no longer committed to the wider NPT regime — straining the treaty's core bargain that non-nuclear states forgo weapons in exchange for P5 progress on disarmament.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: If non-nuclear states conclude the NPT's central bargain is broken, it raises long-term proliferation risk as more countries may question the value of staying non-nuclear.
insights.locator: The grand bargain at the heart of the NPT

insights.claim: Despite the overall failure, non-nuclear states pushed back against weakened disarmament language and showed strong opposition to any resumption of nuclear testing, defending the Comprehensive Test Ban Treaty.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Continued non-nuclear-state engagement is a modest positive signal that the broader NPT membership still values the regime, even if the P5 do not.
insights.locator: There were still meaningful signals from the wider membership

themes.theme: Historical precedent and the path forward

insights.claim: Ahead of the 1985 Review Conference, nuclear arsenals were near their Cold War height and Israel had destroyed Iraq's safeguarded nuclear reactor, yet the United States and Soviet Union set their differences aside to cooperate and reach consensus rather than weaponizing the process — showing cooperative behaviour during high geopolitical tension is possible.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The 1985 precedent is offered as proof that superpower cooperation on non-proliferation is achievable even amid severe tension, setting a bar for what P5 leadership could look like today.
insights.locator: In the leadup to the 1985 Review Conference

insights.claim: Review conferences cannot serve as the primary forum for adjudicating active crises like the wars in Iran or Ukraine, since demanding a consensus-based multilateral process take sides on contentious regional issues makes deadlock almost guaranteed.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Implies future NPT reform should structurally separate crisis-adjudication from treaty-review functions to avoid repeat failures.
insights.locator: An important lesson is that review conferences cannot be the primary forum

insights.claim: The P5 need to strengthen engagement with one another on nuclear risk reduction through the ongoing 'P5 process' diplomatic forum, which has stalled in recent years but remains a crucial route for progress on confidence-building measures around doctrine, new technologies and crisis communication.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The P5 process is the concrete diplomatic channel to watch for any near-term de-escalation signal, since the NPT review mechanism itself is now largely gridlocked until 2031.
insights.locator: The P5 need to strengthen engagement with one another

insights.claim: The next NPT Review Conference is in 2031, and the risk is that underlying conditions deteriorate further in the intervening five years, proliferation pressures mount, and the case for investing political capital in the NPT becomes progressively harder to make.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A five-year gap until the next review conference, with no interim fix in place, is itself a proliferation risk window worth monitoring for ad hoc P5 or bilateral arms-control developments.
insights.locator: The next NPT Review Conference is in 2031
